title: scenario_deepfake_scam
description: Attackers use AI-generated deepfake audio and video to impersonate an executive, convincing staff to authorise a large fraudulent transfer during a fake video call.

timeline.title: Unexpected Video Call Invitation
timeline.description: A finance officer receives a calendar invite for an urgent video call with the CFO and two unknown 'external auditors'.

timeline.title: Deepfake CFO Appears On-Screen
timeline.description: On the call, a realistic AI-generated video and voice of the CFO instructs the officer to transfer funds immediately.

timeline.title: Pressure Applied
timeline.description: The fake CFO insists the transfer is time-sensitive for a regulatory settlement and must not be discussed with others.

timeline.title: Transfer Nearly Processed
timeline.description: The finance officer begins preparing the transfer before a colleague raises doubts about the unusual request.

timeline.title: Real CFO Reached
timeline.description: The officer calls the real CFO's mobile number and confirms the video call was fraudulent.

timeline.title: Incident Reported and Reviewed
timeline.description: The fraudulent call is reported to cybercrime authorities and an internal review is launched into deepfake detection procedures.

node-1.title: Urgent Video Call from the CFO
node-1.situation: A finance officer has just finished a video call in which someone appearing and sounding exactly like the CFO, alongside two individuals introduced as external auditors, requested an urgent wire transfer of £250,000 to a new account. The officer was told this relates to a confidential regulatory settlement and must not be shared with other colleagues. The video and voice quality seemed perfectly normal.
node-1.question: What should the finance officer do before taking any action?

options.label: A
options.text: Do not process any transfer. Independently contact the real CFO via their known mobile number to verify whether the call was genuine.

options.label: B
options.text: Process the transfer immediately — the CFO's face and voice were clearly visible on camera.

options.label: C
options.text: Reply to the video call invitation email to ask for written authorisation before transferring.

node-2.title: CFO Confirms Call Was Fake
node-2.situation: The real CFO confirms they never made such a call. The video call appears to have used an AI-generated deepfake of the CFO's face and voice, built from publicly available video and audio from past company presentations. The email invitation came from a spoofed internal domain.
node-2.question: What should the organisation do to detect deepfake impersonation attempts in future calls?

options.label: A
options.text: Establish a pre-shared code word procedure: for high-value requests on video calls, executives use a secret passphrase only known to core staff to confirm authenticity.

options.label: B
options.text: Require that all internal video calls use the official conferencing platform — this alone will prevent deepfakes.

node-3.title: Limiting Deepfake Training Data
node-3.situation: The attacker built the deepfake from publicly posted conference recordings, earnings call videos, and interviews featuring the CFO. This publicly available content provided sufficient training material.
node-3.question: What policy change reduces the availability of material for executive deepfakes?

options.label: A
options.text: Review and limit the amount of executive audio and video publicly posted. Add watermarks or metadata to official recordings to aid future authenticity verification.

options.label: B
options.text: Prohibit executives from appearing on any public video content entirely.

node-4.title: Payment Authorisation Controls
node-4.situation: The finance team had no policy specifically covering requests made via video call. All large payment authorisations relied on email and phone — video calls were treated as equivalent to an in-person meeting.
node-4.question: What policy change should govern high-value requests made via video calls?

options.label: A
options.text: Update the payment authorisation policy to require dual written sign-off and an independent out-of-band call verification regardless of how the initial request was made, including video calls.

options.label: B
options.text: Only apply extra verification to requests made by email — video calls are harder to fake.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: No fraudulent transfer was made. A code word authentication procedure has been established for high-value requests, payment authorisation policy has been updated to require dual written approval, and staff have received training on recognising and responding to AI deepfake scams.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
